segments.s577-g65: همان is a demonstrative ('that' / 'that very') placed before the noun phrase for emphasis. خامش is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning 'silent' (compare modern خاموش) and آگنده is an adjective meaning 'filled/laden' placed after the noun in classical word order; together they form the noun phrase 'that very filled silence'. باید is the modal verb 'must/ought' that follows the nominal phrase here to express necessity. به + کاه is a prepositional phrase ('onto/with straw') giving the manner or place/result of the action.
segments.s577-g66: بدان is the imperative/hortative 'know/consider it' (ب- + دان) used to mark the following purpose clause. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'). نجوید is a 3rd-person form of جست‌/جویدن here functioning as a negative/jussive ('may not seek' / 'will not seek'), and کس in this construction has the sense 'no one' (i.e. nobody will seek). این پایگاه is the object phrase 'this stronghold/base' that the purpose clause concerns.
segments.s577-g67: بیاویختند is a past-tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they hung down/they suspended' (bi- prefix + آوردن-type form in poetry); از در here means 'from the gate' where در in older usage can mean 'gate/door'. شارستان is the noun 'city/town' and is the source/location from which the hanging occurred.
segments.s577-g68: دگر here means 'another / elsewhere' indicating a different location. پیش is the preposition 'in front of / before' and governs the following noun. دیوار بیمارستان is the noun phrase 'the wall of the hospital' (دیوار = wall, بیمارستان = hospital) giving the second location where the hanging took place.
segments.s577-g69: جهانی is a nominative/agent phrase meaning 'the whole world' or 'all people'. برو آفرین is an idiomatic exclamation in classical Persian (literally 'go, bravo') used as a shout of praise — آفرین functions here as the acclamation 'bravo' and برو is part of the fixed exclamation rather than a literal imperative 'go'. خواندند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they cried/uttered/called' describing the act of shouting this acclamation.
segments.s577-g70: همی is a frequent poetic particle (here an emphatic adverb, roughly 'then/indeed') that precedes the action. خاک بر کشته is a fixed collocation 'dust upon the slain' where خاک = dust and کشته = the slain (people). افشاندند is past 3rd-person plural 'they scattered/sprinkled', so the clause means 'they sprinkled dust on the slain'.
segments.s577-g25: فرو ماند is a past tense verbal phrase (literally 'fell down/was left') used idiomatically here to mean 'fell silent' or 'was struck dumb'; the subject مانی (Mani) follows the verb, a common poetic inversion. میان سخن is an adverbial phrase 'in the middle of the speech' that locates the action.
segments.s577-g26: به گفتار موبد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/by the words/speech of the mobad (Zoroastrian priest)', with به marking the relation 'by/in the speech of'. ز دین کهن is a following genitive/qualifying prepositional phrase 'concerning/from the ancient religion' that specifies the topic of the mobad's speech.
segments.s577-g27: بدو گفت is a simple past clause 'he said to him' with بدو as the indirect object ('to him') followed by گفت (said); کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee. مرد صورت‌پرست is the vocative noun phrase 'man who worships forms/images' (صورت + پرست 'worshipper'), i.e. 'idol‑worshipper'.
segments.s577-g28: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'toward/against God' (to God). چرا is the interrogative 'why'. آختی is an archaic/presented past form meaning 'you approached/you dared/you attacked' (here 'you presumed to do'); خیره‌دست is an epithet applied to the addressee, a compound adjective meaning something like 'bold‑handed/presumptuous' that characterizes the action.
segments.s577-g29: کسی کو here functions as a relative phrase 'the one who / he who' (poetic/archaic: کو = who/that); بلند آسمان is the object 'the lofty/high heaven', and آفرید is the past-tense verb 'created' placed at the end as is normal in Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s577-g30: بدو is an indirect-object pronoun 'to him' (i.e. 'for him' or 'of him' depending on reading). در مکان و زمان is an adverbial phrase 'in place and time' (locating existence within space and time). آفرید is the repeated past-tense verb 'created', giving a parallel/clausal balance with the previous clause.
segments.s577-g31: کجا is a rhetorical interrogative 'where / what place' asking rhetorically about the presence of نور و ظلمت (light and darkness). بدو is 'in him/with him' and اندرست (archaic present) means 'is within' or 'is present', so the clause asks 'Where are light and darkness in him?'
segments.s577-g32: ز هر گوهری literally 'from/above every jewel' functions as a comparative prepositional phrase ('compared with every gem'). گوهرش 'his essence/jewel' is the subject (possessive), and برترست is the predicate 'is superior' (third‑person singular), so the line asserts that his essence surpasses every jewel.
segments.s577-g33: This is a nominal list: 'شب و روز' (night and day) joined by 'و' and followed by the modifier phrase 'گردان سپهر بلند'. 'گردان' is an attributive adjective meaning 'revolving/whirling' that qualifies 'سپهر' (the sky/heavens); 'بلند' is a final adjective 'high/lofty' placed after the noun in poetic order.
segments.s577-g34: 'کزویت' = 'کَز + وی + ت' (from whom/of whom) introduces the source; 'پناهست' is a contracted copular phrase meaning 'is a refuge' (پناه + است). The conjunction 'و' links the two predicates; 'زویت' similarly means 'from him/it' and 'گزند' is a nominal predicate 'harm'. The structure is inverted poetic word order: 'From him is refuge, and from him is harm.'
segments.s577-g35: 'همه کرده' is a noun phrase meaning 'all (the) deeds' or 'everything done'. 'کردگارست' is the predicate: 'کردگار' (the Maker/Creator) plus the copula 'است' contracted into 'ست' → 'is the Creator'. 'و بس' is a short addendum meaning 'and that is enough' or 'and that is all'.
segments.s577-g36: This clause expresses exception and inability: read as 'جز (جزو) ... نتواند ... کس' → 'no one can do this except (Him)'. 'جزو' functions as an exceptive marker here, 'کرد' is the verb 'to do', 'نتواند' is the negative present ('cannot'), 'این کرده' is the direct object 'this deed', and 'کس' is 'anyone' (rendered negative by the verb). Word order is inverted poetically: object before subject.
segments.s577-g37: 'به برهان صورت' is a prepositional/prepositional-like phrase meaning 'by/for the proof of appearance' — i.e., 'for the sake of outward appearance as proof'. 'چرا' (why) introduces the question, and 'بگروی' is the 2nd-person singular present of 'گرویدن' (to adopt, esp. a belief), with the verbal prefix 'بـ' marking present/continuous aspect in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s577-g38: 'همی' is an adverb meaning 'still/continually' placed before the verb. 'پند دین‌آوران' is the object 'the advice/admonition of the religion-bringers' and 'نشنوی' is 2nd-person singular present negative of 'شنیدن' (you do not hear/heed). Together: 'You still do not heed the advice of the religion-bringers.'
segments.s577-g39: A copular sentence listing items: 'همه' (all) modifies the list 'جفت و همتا و یزدان' (pairs, counterparts, and the Divine). 'یکیست' = 'یکی است' (is one) is the predicate asserting unity; the poetic contraction 'یکیست' fuses 'one' and 'is'.
segments.s577-g40: 'جز از بندگی کردنت' is an exceptive prepositional phrase: 'جز' (except) + 'از' (from) + 'بندگی کردنت' ('your servitude' — 'کردنت' is the verbal noun 'your doing/serving'). 'رای نیست' is a negative existential: 'there is no counsel/opinion'. Together: 'There is no counsel except (that of) serving you.'
segments.s577-g1: This is a literary word order: 'ز شاهیش بگذشت پنجاه سال' = 'Fifty years passed of his kingship.' ز (an archaic/poetic form of از) introduces the noun شاھی‑ش (شاه + possessive ‑ش = 'his reign'); بگذشت is the past 3rd‑singular verb 'passed' and سال is the counted noun 'year(s)'.
segments.s577-g2: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause. اندر زمانه = 'in that time/age' (اندر = in), نبودش is نبود + pronominal suffix ‑ش ('there was not for him' / 'there was not'), and همال here functions as 'equal/peer' (an archaic predicate after نبودش). The whole clause means 'for in that age there was no equal to him.'
segments.s577-g3: بیامد is the past 3rd‑singular 'came.' The phrase یکی مرد گویا ز چین is a typical Persian noun phrase 'a certain (یکی) eloquent (گویا) man (مرد) from China (ز چین)', with the epithets following or preceding the noun as idiomatic.
segments.s577-g4: که introduces a relative clause describing the man. چون = 'like/as' introduces the comparison; او مصور نبیند زمین is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic-presentational line: مصور 'painter' as the predicate noun and نبیند (negated present/subjunctive form of دیدن used poetically) meaning 'the world has not seen'—so 'the earth had not seen a painter like him.'
segments.s577-g5: بدان here functions as a fused prepositional/intensifying particle 'with that/by that' (a poetic/archaic use). چربه‌دستی is a compound meaning literally 'greasy hand' but idiomatically 'flattery, bribery, or skilful manipulation'; رسیده is the past participle/predicate 'has reached' or 'attained' and به کام = 'to (his) desire' means 'fulfilled his wish.'
segments.s577-g6: یکی introduces 'a certain' person; برمنش is an epithet 'of noble disposition' placed before مرد 'man'; مانی is the proper name; به نام is a conventional formula 'by the name (of)' = 'named.' Together: 'a noble‑minded man named Mani.'
segments.s577-g7: به صورتگری گفت is 'he said to the painter/portrait‑maker' (به + indirect object). گفت is the past verb 'said'; پیغمبرم is a predicative nominal with the first‑person suffix ‑م ('I am a prophet') — in reported speech the utterance follows the verb.
segments.s577-g8: ز here functions as the archaic/poetic form of از meaning 'than' or 'over' in comparisons. دین‌آوران جهان = 'the religion‑bringers of the world' (دین‌آوران = those who bring a religion), and برترم is the adjective برتر 'superior' with the first‑person suffix ‑م, 'I am superior.' The whole phrase is 'I am superior to the religion‑bringers of the world.'
segments.s577-g49: نگنجد is a negative intransitive verb (3rd singular) meaning “does not fit/occur”; in this poetic line the verb comes before its subject (inversion). جهان‌آفرین is the subject (a compound noun = “world‑creator”), and در گمان is a prepositional phrase “in (the) thought/mind” modifying the verb.
segments.s577-g50: که introduces the subordinate clause “that …”. او is the pronoun subject “he”; برترست is a predicative adjective/verb phrase “is superior” (older form with -ست). از marks the comparative relation (“than / above”), followed by the two nouns زمان and مکان conjoined by و (“time and place”).
segments.s577-g51: سخنهای is the plural noun “words/sayings.” دیوانگانست is a predicative phrase meaning “are (the) madmen’s” or “belong to madmen” (here: ‘are the words of madmen’). و بس is a short appended phrase “and that is enough” (بس = enough).
segments.s577-g52: بدین‌بر is an adverbial phrase “in this way / regarding this.” نباشد is a negative jussive/optative 3rd‑person form meaning “let there not be” (i.e. “may there be no …”). ترا is the clitic pronoun “to you” formed from ت + را. یار کس literally “companion person” with کس here under negation meaning “no one (to be your companion).” Word order places the jussive verb before the phrase it governs for poetic effect.
segments.s577-g53: سخنها is the noun “words/sayings.” جزین (literally “except this / besides this”) and نیز “also” modify the noun, and بسیار “many” quantifies it. گفت is the past tense verb “he said/uttered” (verb at the end as typical SOV), here used in its simple past 3rd‑person form.
segments.s577-g54: که introduces a relative clause modifying those words. با + دانش و مردمی lists the two groups “with the learned and the people.” بود is the past copula “was,” and جفت is the predicate nominal meaning “a match / pair” — together: “which were a match for the scholars and the populace.”
segments.s577-g55: فرو ماند is a compound: فرو (down/utterly) + ماند (past ‘remained/was left’) giving the sense “was overwhelmed / fell down.” The proper name مانی is the subject (Mani). ز گفتار اوی is an ablative phrase “by/from his speech” (اوی = his), indicating cause.
segments.s577-g56: بپژمرد is a past 3rd‑person verb meaning “withered / was made to wither.” شاداب بازار اوی is the noun phrase “his lively market” (شاداب = lively/fresh, بازار = market, اوی = his). The verb precedes the noun phrase here for metrical/poetic order: “his lively market withered.”
segments.s577-g57: 'ز مانی برآشفت پس شهریار' is a compact, poetic word order: 'ز مانی' (prepositional phrase) = 'because of Mani', 'برآشفت' is the past/preterite verb 'was angered/became angry' (literary form of آشفتن), 'پس' is a connective 'then/thereupon', and 'شهریار' ('the king') is the subject/agent associated with برآشفت. In short: 'Because of Mani, the king became angry.'
segments.s577-g58: In 'برو تنگ شد گردش روزگار' the main clause is 'تنگ شد گردش روزگار' = 'the turning/circuit of day/time (گردش روزگار) became tight/constrained', with تنگ شد a two-word predicate ('tang' + شد = 'grew narrow/constrained'). 'برو' here is a brief poetic particle/marker linking the clause to what precedes (thereupon/then) and is not a full imperative in sense; the phrase overall means 'thereupon the wheel/cycle of fate tightened.'
segments.s577-g59: 'بفرمود' is the honorific/polite past verb 'he said/ordered' (reports a command). 'پس' is the connective 'then', 'تاش' is the object meaning 'an effort/attempt', and 'برداشتند' is past plural 'they took up/undertook' (برداشتن used here as 'take up' an effort). The clause means: 'He ordered, and then they took up the attempt.'
segments.s577-g60: 'به خواری ز درگاه بگذاشتند' is an action phrase: 'به خواری' is an adverbial phrase 'in humiliation/with disgrace', 'ز درگاه' = 'from the court' (ز = 'from'), and 'بگذاشتند' (they left/they put aside/they cast out) is past plural. Together: 'they cast him out of the court in humiliation.' Note the order is poetic (adverbial first, then source, then verb).
segments.s577-g61: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here functions as the past reporting verb 'said'); 'کاین مرد صورت‌پرست' = 'that this man, the صورت‌پرست' (صورت‌پرست is a compound: literally 'form/appearance-worshipper' — either 'idolater' or 'superficial person'). Grammatically کاین introduces the subordinate clause describing the man.
segments.s577-g62: 'نگنجد همی در سرای نشست' contains two predicates in sequence. 'نگنجد' (negative present/subjunctive form of گنجیدن) means 'does not fit / will not be contained' (poetic negative statement). 'همی' is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here 'indeed/still/so'), 'در سرای' = 'in the house/hall/court' (prepositional phrase), and 'نشست' is the past verb 'he sat' or 'he settled'. The clause reads roughly 'does not fit; so he sat in the hall.' Note: سرای is used as 'house/hall' here (poetic noun).
segments.s577-g63: 'چو' is the subordinating conjunction 'when/if/as'. The paired nouns 'آشوب و آرام' ('turmoil and calm') present opposites, and 'گیتی' ('the world') is the domain where these occur; 'به دوست' marks the beneficiary/target 'to/for a friend'. Literally: 'When turmoil and calm (fall) upon the world for a friend...' — introducing a conditional/general statement.
segments.s577-g64: The clause 'بباید کشیدن سراپاش پوست' expresses obligation plus an infinitive: 'بباید' = 'one must/ought to', followed by the verbal noun/infinitive 'کشیدن'. Here کشیدن is best read in the sense 'to endure/suffer'. 'سراپاش' (variant of سراپا) is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'completely/all over', and 'پوست' is 'skin' — figuratively 'to have one's skin pulled' i.e. to endure complete suffering. So: 'one must endure, wholly, being skinned (for a friend).'
segments.s577-g9: ز چین (preposition + place) means 'from China' with ز governing the place name چین before the verb. نزد شاپور شد is a compact past action: نزد + name = 'to/into the presence of Shapur' and شد (past) marks the arrival/occurrence. بار خواست places بار (a 'load/occasion/burden') before the past verb خواست 'he wanted/asked', an idiomatic object + past-verb order common in classical Persian.
segments.s577-g10: به پیغمبری is a prepositional phrase 'to/for prophethood' (به + noun). شاه را uses the definite object marker را to mark شاه as the direct object, and یار خواست is a verb phrase 'he asked (خواست) for an ally/companion (یار)' with the object (شاه را) preceding the verb, the normal classical word order.
segments.s577-g11: سخن گفت is a verb phrase where the noun سخن ('speech/words') comes before گفت (here used as the verbal notion 'spoke/said'); this noun+verb order is common in Persian. مرد گشاده‌زبان is a nominative apposition 'a man, broad-tongued/eloquent', with the adjective compound گشاده‌زبان after the noun.
segments.s577-g12: جهاندار شد is 'the sovereign became' (جهاندار = ruler; شد = became). زان سخن is a prepositional phrase 'from/ because of that speech' (زان = 'from that/for that reason' + سخن). بدگمان here functions as a predicate adjective 'suspicious', so the full clause says the sovereign 'became suspicious because of that speech.'
segments.s577-g13: سرش تیز شد is an idiomatic clause: سر 'head' + the enclitic ش ('his') + تیز 'sharp/keen' + شد 'became' = 'his head grew sharp' (i.e., he became alert/angry). موبدان را بخواند places the object (موبدان with the object-marker را) before the verb بخواند; here بخواند means 'he summoned/called' the priests.
segments.s577-g14: زمانی فراوان is an adverbial phrase 'for a long/ample time' (زمانی = 'for a while' / 'a time' and فراوان 'abundantly', here adverbial). سخنها (plural 'words/speeches') is the topic and براند (from راند) is used here in the sense 'utter/say/drive forth' — literally 'they drove forth words', i.e. 'they spoke many words'. The verb follows its plural subject/topic, typical in classical narrative.
segments.s577-g15: کزین مرد is a prepositional/relative phrase 'from this man' (کزین = 'from this/thereof' + مرد 'man'). چینی identifies his origin ('Chinese'), and the conjunction و links to چیره‌زبان, an adjective compound meaning 'masterful‑tongued' (here with the connotation 'glib' or 'smooth‑talking'). The adjectives follow the noun they modify.
segments.s577-g16: فتادستم is a past-tense intransitive verbal compound meaning 'befell me' or 'happened to me' (literally 'fell upon me'). از دین او در گمان is a short prepositional phrase 'in suspicion concerning his religion' (از ... دین = 'concerning his religion'; او = 'him'; در گمان = 'in suspicion/doubt'), so the whole clause states that suspicion regarding his religion came upon the speaker.
segments.s577-g17: بگویید is a 2nd-person plural (or polite) imperative/optative meaning “speak/say.” The structure places the imperative verb first, followed by the coordinating conjunction و هم (“and also”), then the poetic contraction زو (از + او = “from him”) and the object سخن (“speech/words”). بشنوید is another 2nd-person plural imperative “hear/listen.” The whole segment commands both to speak and to hear the man’s words.
segments.s577-g18: مگر here introduces an exception/conditional sense “unless” (classical usage). خود is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun “yourselves/your own,” and به گفتار او بگروید is a verb phrase with به + noun meaning “to adhere to/accept his speech,” بگروید being the present/subjunctive/optative form of گرویدن “to adopt/join.” Together the clause reads as a proviso: do not accept him except if you yourselves adopt his words.
segments.s577-g19: بگفتند is simple past, 3rd-person plural “they said” (or a narrative ‘it was said’). کین is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative (classical Persian) roughly “this/that” (not represented in the provided senses list). مرد is the noun “man,” and صورت پرست is a compound noun: صورت (“form/image”) + پرست (“-worshipper”), i.e. “image-worshipper” or “idolater.” The phrase reports what people said about the man.
segments.s577-g20: نه begins the negation “not.” بر here means “on/upon / according to” (used idiomatically as ‘according to’ or ‘by’), followed by مایه موبدان “the m ā y e (basis/teaching) of the mobeds (priests).” موبه دست is a collocation: موبه (literally “hair by hair” / “minutely”) + دست (“hand”), i.e. “with exacting/fastidious hand.” The whole segment denies conformity to the mobeds’ measured/precise rites.
segments.s577-g21: زمانی is an adverb here “for a while/for the time being.” سخن بشنو is an imperative sequence: سخن (object “speech/words”) + بشنو (2nd-person singular/plural imperative “listen/hear”). او را contains the object pronoun او (“him”) plus the definite/object marker را, and بخوان is another imperative form meaning “call/summon” or “recite” depending on context (here likely “summon him”); the lemma senses provided list only ‘read/recite’ and ‘sing,’ so the exact sense in the list is not available.
segments.s577-g22: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” بیند (3rd-singular present/subjunctive of دیدن) means “he sees” or “he meets,” and ورا (ورا = از + او / to him) is the indirect object. کی here is the interrogative “who” (not ‘when’), and گشاید is a 3rd-singular present/subjunctive meaning “will open” (classical/conjunctive form of گشودن). زبان is used metonymically for “speech” or “utterance.” The clause asks rhetorically who will speak when he sees (them).
segments.s577-g23: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning “he commanded/said” (literal: ‘he said/ordered’). تا introduces a purpose/result clause “so that / in order that,” here linking the order to its effect. موبد آمدش پیش is a compact clause: موبد “the mobed/priest,” آمدش (آمد + ـش) “came to him,” and پیش “in the presence of / before (him).” Word order places the verb after the subject in classical narrative style.
segments.s577-g24: سخن گفت is the verb phrase “spoke/said words” (سخن as object/nominal, گفت as past 3rd-person of گفتن). با او is the prepositional phrase “with him” and ز اندازه بیش is an idiomatic adverbial “beyond measure / more than is proper” (ز used poetically as ‘from/of’ plus اندازه ‘measure/degree’ and بیش ‘more’). The clause thus reports that the mobed spoke with him at excessive length/degree.
segments.s577-g41: This is a clause built around the noun phrase 'گرین صورت' (a tearful/weepy face) followed by the perfective participle 'کرده' ('having made' / 'having put on') and the present/subjunctive form 'کنی' ('you do' / 'you would do'). The word 'جنبان' functions as a verbal/adjectival element 'moving; making a show of motion' and links to 'کنی' (you act so). Word order is noun phrase + participle + verbal phrase, a common classical Persian compact way to say 'if you put on X and act so.'
segments.s577-g42: The clause begins with 'سزد‌گر' — a fused poetic expression meaning roughly 'it would be fitting / appropriate if' (sزد + گر as a conditional particle). 'ز' is the preposition 'from/of', 'جنبده' here means 'a living creature' (جنبنده), and 'برهان کنی' is a verbal phrase 'you produce/bring proof' with 'کنی' again in the present/subjunctive. Together: 'it would be fitting if you could bring proof from a living being.'
segments.s577-g43: 'ندانی' is the second-person singular present 'you do not know'. It introduces a content clause with 'که' ('that'). Inside the clause 'برهان نیاید به کار' has the verb 'نیاید' (negative of آمدن in subjunctive/optative sense: 'will not come / will not be available') and the prepositional phrase 'به کار' ('to/for use' — i.e. 'be of use'). So: 'you do not know that proof will not be of use.'
segments.s577-g44: This is a verbless/nominal clause framed with the negative verb 'ندارد' ('he/it does not have' or 'there is no [one who has]'). 'کسی' functions as an indefinite subject ('anyone' in neutral form but rendered 'no one' in this negative context). 'این سخن استوار' is the object/predicate ('this saying as firm/stable'). Together: 'No one holds this saying to be firm.'
segments.s577-g45: A conditional protasis beginning with 'اگر' ('if'). 'اهرمن' is the subject (Ahriman), 'جفت یزدان' is a predicate phrase meaning 'the pair/peer of (the) God' (جفت = pair/peer, یزدان = God). The final word 'بدی' here functions as an archaic/copulative verbal form ('would be') used in poetic diction instead of an explicit modern copula; it is not the noun 'evil' in this context.
segments.s577-g46: Parallel predicate clause: 'شب تیره' (night + adjective 'dark') followed by the comparative/linker 'چون' ('as' / 'like') and 'روز خندان' (day + adjective 'smiling/bright'). The clause closes with the same archaic copula 'بدی' ('would be'), so the construction says that night would be dark and day would be smiling if the prior condition held.
segments.s577-g47: 'همه‌ساله' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'all year long'. 'بودی' is again the poetic/copulative past/subjunctive form of 'to be' ('would be' / 'would exist'). 'شب و روز راست' lists the coordinated nouns 'night and day' with 'راست' ('right/regular/straight') qualifying them — here meaning 'continuous/steady'. The clause says that night and day would continue all year.
segments.s577-g48: 'به گردش' ('by/with the turning/cycle') is an adverbial phrase referring to the revolutions of time. 'فزونی' is 'increase' and stands as the first term; 'نبودی' is the negative of the same copular/auxiliary form 'would not be' (so: 'there would not be increase'). 'نه کاست' adds 'nor (کاست) decrease' — 'کاست' here functions as 'diminution/decrease'. Together: 'by their turning there would be neither increase nor decrease.'

sentences.s577-s33: Consider that same silence, full (of something), and let it be laid on straw so that no one will seek this stronghold.
sentences.s577-s34: They hung (them) from the city's gate; elsewhere (they hung them) before the wall of the hospital.
sentences.s577-s35: The whole world cried out 'Bravo!'; they then scattered dust upon the slain.
sentences.s577-s13: Mani fell silent in the midst of the mobad's speech about the ancient faith.
sentences.s577-s14: He said to him, 'O man who worships images, why did you presume to act so against God, bold‑handed one?'
sentences.s577-s15: He who created the lofty heaven created him within place and time.
sentences.s577-s16: What place has light and darkness in him? His essence is superior to every jewel.
sentences.s577-s17: Night and day and the whirling high heavens — from him is refuge, and from him is harm.
sentences.s577-s18: All that has been done is the work of the Creator — and that is enough; no one but He can have done this deed.
sentences.s577-s19: Why do you adopt outward form as a proof? You still do not heed the admonitions of the religion-bringers.
sentences.s577-s20: All pairs and counterparts and the Divine are one; apart from servitude to you there is no counsel.
sentences.s577-s1: Fifty years of his kingship passed, for in that age there was no equal to him.
sentences.s577-s2: There came a certain eloquent man from China, such that the earth had not seen a painter like him.
sentences.s577-s3: By that oily hand (i.e. by that flattery or skill) he had attained his desire — a noble‑minded man named Mani.
sentences.s577-s4: He said to the painter, 'I am a prophet; I am superior to the religion‑bringers of the world.'
sentences.s577-s25: It does not enter the mind of the World‑Creator that He is above time and place.
sentences.s577-s26: These are the words of madmen — that is enough; in this matter may you have no companion.
sentences.s577-s27: He uttered many words besides this as well, which were a match for both the learned and the people.
sentences.s577-s28: Mani was overwhelmed by his speech; his lively market withered.
sentences.s577-s29: Because of Mani the king grew angry; then the wheel of fate tightened.
sentences.s577-s30: He commanded, and then they made an attempt; they cast him out of the court in humiliation.
sentences.s577-s31: Thus he said that this man, a worshipper of appearance, does not fit; then he sat in the hall.
sentences.s577-s32: When turmoil and calm come upon the world, one must, for a friend, endure being skinned all over.
sentences.s577-s5: A man from China came to Shapur and made demands; he asked the king to be his companion in prophethood.
sentences.s577-s6: An eloquent man spoke; the sovereign became suspicious because of that speech.
sentences.s577-s7: He grew agitated and summoned the mobeds; for a long time they spoke many words.
sentences.s577-s8: From that Chinese, the smooth‑tongued man, doubt befell me about his religion.
sentences.s577-s9: Speak, and also listen to his words, but do not yourselves accept his teaching.
sentences.s577-s10: They said that this man worships images, not according to the mobeds’ measure nor by the mobeds’ exact hand.
sentences.s577-s11: Listen to his words for a while and summon him; when he sees you, who will open his tongue?
sentences.s577-s12: He commanded that the mobed come forward; the mobed came before him and spoke with him beyond measure.
sentences.s577-s21: If you put on that tearful appearance and make a show of moving about, it would be fitting if you could produce a proof from a living creature.
sentences.s577-s22: You do not know that proof will be of no use; no one considers this saying to be firm.
sentences.s577-s23: If Ahriman were the peer of God, night would be dark and day would be smiling.
sentences.s577-s24: All year round night and day would persist; by their turning there would be neither increase nor decrease.
